en.extract:
The plumbeous forest-falcon (Micrastur plumbeus) is a bird of prey in the family Falconidae only found in the Chocó region in Colombia and Ecuador. This rare bird has not been often spotted, which makes its study complicated. For a long time, it was considered to be part of the lined forest-falcons but it now known to be a species of its own. It is currently considered a vulnerable species because of the precarity of its habitat that is threatened by deforestation.


Description
The adult plumbeous forest-falcon has a grey back and a grey head with very bright orange legs and face. Their breast is barred and paler than their back, and their belly is more whitish towards the tail. This raptor has a black tail with a white tip and a white band in the middle, going across the whole width. Their irides are dark brown. The individuals are 30-34 cm (13 in) long and females are bigger than males. As a matter of fact, females weigh 180-213g and males weigh 172-188g.
Immature individuals look similar besides a whiter breast and beige-ish irides, clearer than in mature individuals.
Forest-falcons usually have long tails and round wings, but the plumbeous forest-falcon has a shorter tail and long wings.


Taxonomy
For some time, it had been debated whether the plumbeous forest-falcon was a lined forest-falcon (Micrastur gilvicollis) or a subspecies of the latter because of their resemblance. As it turned out, these birds were two different species. Their territorial distributions are completely separate.
M. plumbeus and M. ruficollis interstes live in the same environment but do not interbreed, or even interfere with each other. They are sympatric and syntonic. The plumbeous forest-falcon differentiated about 3 million years ago after the split between trans- and cis-Andean South American populations. The closure of the Panama seaway, due to a drier and cooler climate linked to the formation of ice sheets at higher latitudes, may have highly contributed to the isolation of the plumbeous forest-falcon.
Forest-falcons are part of the sub-family Herpetotherinae. This clade's and Polyborinae's species are usually found in forests and wetlands.


Habitat and Distribution
The plumbeous forest-falcon is endemic to the south of the Chocó region in South America. This region extends in the south-west of Colombia and the north-west of Ecuador. This bird lives all-year long in forest lowlands and foothill forests; it is not a migratory bird. The elevation of its habitat varies from the sea level to approximately 1500m of altitude. One research project studying a few pairs of this species found that their territory extended over 35-40 ha.


Conservation status
This species has been classified as vulnerable by the IUCN because of habitat loss. Deforestation is touching more territories and the plumbeous forest-falcon's habitat is being destructed. It is an unprotected species that needs to be studied more extensively to help its conservation. Efforts should be focused on the Chocó area where this organism is solely contained.


Behavior


Vocalizations
Two main types of vocalizations are known for this bird. They produce some yapping kew notes every 1.5 - 2.5s, as well as series of cah cah cah-cah-cah that may be sung by pairs. Three types of calls and songs are known for the forest falcons. The most common one is the "territorial advising song". The plumbeous forest-falcon repeat series of five notes to claim his territory. Furthermore, the "excited cackling call" and the "quacking song" are also heard from Micrastur species, even though less often then the first call. Two members of the same species sometimes sing in a pair.


Diet
Due to the lack of study, the diet of this forest-falcon is not perfectly known. However, it is most probable that it is an opportunistic predator feeding on small vertebrates and large invertebrates. Land crabs, birds and lizards were found in the digestive system of a few individuals. Its main source of food is thought to be lizards. Moreover, this family of birds prey on forest-dwelling birds and rodents.
They have two principal ways to forage. With the first one, they remain on the ground while running after the prey that they eventually catch with their feet. They keep their tail high when they chase the prey. The second technique consists of being perched about 2m above the ground, looking for sounds and movements in the leaf litter and then jumping on the detected prey.


Reproduction
The plumbeous forest-falcon's reproduction is not really known but it probably sets up its nests in tree cavities. As a matter of fact, other members of the Micrastur family that have been more studied, such as the collared forest-falcon and the barred forest-falcon, nest in trees.


References


External links
Photo of the plumbeous forest-falcon on eBird
Songs and calls of the plumbeous forest-falcon on xeno-canto

The '''plumbeous forest-falcon''' ('''''Micrastur plumbeus''''') is a [[bird of prey]] in the family [[Falconidae]] only found in the Chocó region in [[Colombia]] and [[Ecuador]]. This rare bird has not been often spotted, which makes its study complicated. For a long time, it was considered to be part of the [[Lined forest falcon|lined forest-falcons]] but it now known to be a species of its own.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|last1=Bierregaard|first1=Richard O.|last2=Boesman|first2=Peter F. D.|last3=Marks|first3=Jeffrey S.|date=2020-03-04|title=Plumbeous Forest-Falcon (Micrastur plumbeus)|url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/plffal1/cur/introduction|journal=Birds of the World|language=en}}</ref> It is currently considered a vulnerable species because of the precarity of its habitat that is threatened by [[deforestation]].<ref name=":3">{{Cite journal|last=Bierregaard|first=RO|date=1998|title=Conservation status of birds of prey in the South American tropics|url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/jrr/v032n01/p00019-p00027.pdf|journal=Journal of Raptor Research|volume=32|page=19|via=SORA journals}}</ref>

== Description ==
The adult plumbeous forest-falcon has a grey back and a grey head with very bright orange legs and face. Their breast is barred and paler than their back, and their belly is more whitish towards the tail. This raptor has a black tail with a white tip and a white band in the middle, going across the whole width. Their [[Iris (anatomy)|irides]] are dark brown. The individuals are 30-34 cm (13 in) long and females are bigger than males.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last1=Ferguson-Lees|first1=James|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hlIztc05HTQC&dq=raptors+of+the+world&pg=PA6|title=Raptors of the World|last2=Christie|first2=David A.|date=2001|publisher=Houghton Mifflin Harcourt|isbn=978-0-618-12762-7|language=en}}</ref> As a matter of fact, females weigh 180-213g and males weigh 172-188g.<ref name=":0" />

Immature individuals look similar besides a whiter breast<ref name=":1" /> and beige-ish irides, clearer than in mature individuals.<ref name=":2">{{Cite journal|last=WHITTAKER|first=ANDREW|title=A New Species of Forest-Falcon (Falconidae: Micrastur) from Southeastern Amazonia and the Atlantic Rainforests of Brazil|date=December 2002|url=https://bioone.org/journals/the-wilson-bulletin/volume-114/issue-4/0043-5643_2002_114_0421_ANSOFF_2.0.CO_2/A-NEW-SPECIES-OF-FOREST-FALCON-FALCONIDAE--MICRASTUR-FROM/10.1676/0043-5643(2002)114%5B0421:ANSOFF%5D2.0.CO;2.full|journal=The Wilson Bulletin|language=en|volume=114|issue=4|page=421|doi=10.1676/0043-5643(2002)114[0421:ansoff]2.0.co;2|issn=0043-5643}}</ref>

[[Forest falcon|Forest-falcons]] usually have long tails and round wings, but the plumbeous forest-falcon has a shorter tail and long wings.<ref name=":4">{{Cite book|last=S.|first=Fowler, Murray E. Cubas, Zalmir|title=Biology, medicine, and surgery of South American wild animals|date=2001|publisher=Iowa State University Press|isbn=0-8138-2846-5|oclc=45419037}}</ref>

== Taxonomy ==
For some time, it had been debated whether the plumbeous forest-falcon was a lined forest-falcon (''Micrastur gilvicollis'') or a [[subspecies]] of the latter because of their resemblance.<ref name=":0" /> As it turned out, these birds were two different species. Their territorial distributions are completely separate.

''M. plumbeus'' and ''M. ruficollis interstes'' live in the same environment but do not [[Hybrid (biology)|interbreed]], or even interfere with each other. They are [[Sympatric speciation|sympatric]] and syntonic.<ref name=":2" /> The plumbeous forest-falcon differentiated about 3 million years ago after the split between trans- and cis-Andean South American populations.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Soares|first1=Leonardo Moura dos Santos|last2=Bates|first2=John|last3=Carneiro|first3=Lincoln Silva|last4=Santos|first4=Marcos Pérsio Dantas|last5=Aleixo|first5=Alexandre|date=April 2019|title=Molecular systematics, biogeography and taxonomy of forest‐falcons in the Micrastur ruficollis species complex (Aves: Falconidae)|journal=Journal of Avian Biology|volume=50|issue=4|doi=10.1111/jav.01943|s2cid=109070719|issn=0908-8857}}</ref> The closure of the Panama seaway, due to a drier and cooler climate linked to the formation of [[Ice sheet|ice sheets]] at higher latitudes, may have highly contributed to the isolation of the plumbeous forest-falcon.<ref name=":5">{{Cite journal|last1=Fuchs|first1=Jérôme|last2=Chen|first2=Steven|last3=Johnson|first3=Jeff A.|last4=Mindell|first4=David P.|date=September 2011|title=Pliocene diversification within the South American Forest falcons (Falconidae: Micrastur)|journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution|volume=60|issue=3|pages=398–407|doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2011.05.008|pmid=21621621|issn=1055-7903}}</ref>

Forest-falcons are part of the sub-family [[Herpetotherinae]]. This clade's and [[Caracara (subfamily)|Polyborinae]]'s species are usually found in forests and [[Wetland|wetlands]].<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Fuchs|first1=Jérôme|last2=Johnson|first2=Jeff A.|last3=Mindell|first3=David P.|date=January 2015|title=Rapid diversification of falcons (Aves: Falconidae) due to expansion of open habitats in the Late Miocene|journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution|volume=82|pages=166–182|doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2014.08.010|pmid=25256056|issn=1055-7903}}</ref>

== Habitat and Distribution ==
The plumbeous forest-falcon is [[Endemism|endemic]] to the south of the Chocó region in [[South America]]. This region extends in the south-west of Colombia and the north-west of Ecuador. This bird lives all-year long in forest lowlands and [[Foothills|foothill]] forests; it is not a [[migratory bird]]. The elevation of its habitat varies from the [[sea level]] to approximately 1500m of altitude.<ref name=":0" /> One research project studying a few pairs of this species found that their [[Territory (animal)|territory]] extended over 35-40 ha.<ref name=":2" />

=== Conservation status ===
This species has been classified as [[Vulnerable species|vulnerable]] by the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature|IUCN]] because of [[Habitat destruction|habitat loss]].<ref name=":0" /> Deforestation is touching more territories and the plumbeous forest-falcon's habitat is being destructed. It is an unprotected species that needs to be studied more extensively to help its conservation.<ref name=":3" /> Efforts should be focused on the Chocó area where this organism is solely contained.<ref name=":5" />

== Behavior ==

=== Vocalizations ===
{{Birdsong|species=the plumbeous forest-falcon|url=https://www.xeno-canto.org/species/Micrastur-plumbeus|image=MicrasturPlumbeusGronvold.jpg}}Two main types of vocalizations are known for this bird. They produce some yapping ''kew'' notes every 1.5 - 2.5s, as well as series of ''cah cah cah-cah-cah'' that may be sung by pairs.<ref name=":0" /> Three types of [[Bird vocalization|calls]] and [[Bird vocalization|songs]] are known for the forest falcons. The most common one is the "territorial advising song". The plumbeous forest-falcon repeat series of five notes to claim his territory. Furthermore, the "excited cackling call" and the "quacking song" are also heard from ''[[Forest falcon|Micrastur]]'' species, even though less often then the first call. Two members of the same species sometimes sing in a pair.<ref name=":2" />

=== Diet ===
Due to the lack of study, the diet of this forest-falcon is not perfectly known. However, it is most probable that it is an [[Opportunism|opportunistic]] [[Predation|predator]] feeding on small [[Vertebrate|vertebrates]] and large [[Invertebrate|invertebrates]]. Land [[Crab|crabs]], [[Bird|birds]] and [[Lizard|lizards]] were found in the digestive system of a few individuals.<ref name=":0" /> Its main source of food is thought to be lizards.<ref name=":2" /> Moreover, this family of birds prey on forest-dwelling birds and [[Rodent|rodents]].<ref name=":4" />

They have two principal ways to forage. With the first one, they remain on the ground while running after the prey that they eventually catch with their feet. They keep their tail high when they chase the prey. The second technique consists of being perched about 2m above the ground, looking for sounds and movements in the [[Plant litter|leaf litter]] and then jumping on the detected prey.<ref name=":2" />

=== Reproduction ===
The plumbeous forest-falcon's [[reproduction]] is not really known but it probably sets up its [[Nest|nests]] in tree cavities.<ref name=":0" /> As a matter of fact, other members of the ''Micrastur'' family that have been more studied, such as the [[Collared forest falcon|collared forest-falcon]] and the [[Barred forest falcon|barred forest-falcon]], nest in trees.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Thorstrom|first1=Russell K.|last2=Turley|first2=Charles W.|last3=Ramirez|first3=Feliciano Gutierrez|last4=Gilroy|first4=Beth Ann|date=February 1990|title=Descriptions of Nests, Eggs, and Young of the Barred Forest-Falcon (Micrastur ruficollis) and of the Collared Forest-Falcon (M. semitorquatus)|journal=The Condor|volume=92|issue=1|pages=237–239|doi=10.2307/1368405|jstor=1368405|issn=0010-5422}}</ref>
